serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a typical large cup of iced lemon tea served at a Singapore kopitiam or bing sutt.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon tea, ready-to-drink, sweetened — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (sweetened iced lemon tea)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'dong ning cha' (Hong Kong-style iced lemon tea) exists in HPB or USDA. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 14355 (tea, ready-to-drink, lemon-flavoured, sweetened) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for sweetened cold tea beverages. Sugar content reflects typical kopitiam preparation with approximately 8–10 g sugar per 100 ml. Lemon juice contribution to vitamin C is estimated from USDA lemon juice data (NDB 09152) at typical dilution.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened beverages — lemon-flavoured sweetened tea, analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened cold tea beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Hong Kong-style iced lemon tea (dong ning cha). GI estimated at 56 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sucrose-sweetened cold tea beverages and lemon-flavoured sweetened drinks, which cluster in the medium GI range (GI 50–65). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (56 × 29.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.7.

hypertension.guidance: Iced lemon tea is low in sodium (~17.5 mg per 350 ml serving) and does not meaningfully contribute to hypertensive risk from a sodium standpoint; however, the added sugar content should be monitored as excess sugar intake is associated with elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content and medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 350 ml serving delivers approximately 29 g of added sugar and a glycemic load of ~16.7, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit sweetened iced lemon tea and consider unsweetened alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake linked to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as iced lemon tea is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and prefer unsweetened tea options.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 招牌冻柠茶 (Zhao Pai Dong Ning Cha), known in English as Signature Iced Lemon Tea, is a popular cold beverage served at Hong Kong-style bing sutts (ice rooms) and Singapore kopitiams. It is prepared by brewing strong black tea, chilling it, and serving it over ice with freshly squeezed or sliced lemon and sugar syrup. The drink is characterised by a bright, tangy lemon flavour balanced against the mild bitterness of black tea, with a refreshing sweetness. The 'signature' (招牌) designation at establishments such as Keming Bing Sat indicates a house-style preparation that may use a proprietary tea blend or specific lemon-to-sugar ratio. Nutritionally, a standard 350 ml serving provides approximately 119 kcal, almost entirely from added sugar (~29 g carbohydrate). Fat and protein content are negligible. The beverage has a medium glycemic index (~56) and a glycemic load of approximately 16.7 per serving, making it a significant source of rapidly absorbed carbohydrates. It is low in sodium and contains a small amount of vitamin C from lemon juice. Clinically, it is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome due to its high sugar content. It is vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free. This drink is a staple of Singaporean and Hong Kong Chinese café culture and is widely available across hawker centres, kopitiams, and bing sutts throughout Singapore.

black tea: Hong Kong milk tea base (without milk)

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hong Kong-style iced lemon tea (dong ning cha) exists in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA NDB 14355 (sweetened lemon-flavoured ready-to-drink tea) and HPB Singapore sweetened cold tea data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sucrose-sweetened cold tea beverages. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
